questions.question: Colonial economic policies created a skewed distribution of income and wealth in Indian society. Which of the following is a key indicator of income inequality in colonial India?
questions.solution:
Income Inequality in Colonial India

Colonial economic policies resulted in unequal land ownership patterns as a key indicator of inequality. Systems like the Permanent Settlement created a class of wealthy zamindars while the actual tillers of the land (peasants) remained impoverished, landless, and burdened by debt.

questions.question: What was the ultimate promise made by Bhagirath Majhi to the Santhals if they followed his teachings?
questions.option_4: Access to modern education and jobs
questions.solution:
Bhagirath Majhi and the Santhals

Bhagirath Majhi, the leader of the Kherwar Movement, promised the Santhals the restoration of tribal kingdoms. He preached that their lands belonged to them by divine right and that by purifying their lifestyle and following his teachings, they would regain their lost sovereignty from the British and zamindars.

questions.question: Where was the headquarters of the Home Rule League founded by Bal Gangadhar Tilak located?
questions.solution:
Tilak’s Home Rule League

The headquarters of the Home Rule League founded by Bal Gangadhar Tilak in April 1916 was located in Pune. His league operated primarily in Maharashtra (excluding Bombay city), Karnataka, the Central Provinces, and Berar, while Annie Besant's league covered the rest of India.

questions.question: The partition of Bengal officially came into force in the year _______.
questions.solution:
Partition of Bengal

The Partition of Bengal, announced by Lord Curzon, officially came into force on October 16, 1905. The day was observed as a day of mourning across Bengal, with people fasting and singing Vande Mataram to protest the administrative division of the province along communal lines.

questions.question: This freedom fighter proclaimed himself the Peshwa during the Revolt of 1857 and gathered armed forces and expelled the British garrison from the city. He was the adopted son of the late Peshwa Baji Rao. The leader in question is:
questions.solution:
Nana Saheb

Nana Saheb, the adopted son of the last Maratha Peshwa Baji Rao II, led the 1857 Revolt in Kanpur. After the British refused to continue his father's pension, he proclaimed himself Peshwa, gathered an army, and successfully forced the British garrison to surrender the city.

questions.question: Which of the following names is associated with the Faraizi Movement?
questions.solution:
Haji Shariatullah

The Faraizi Movement was founded by Haji Shariatullah in 1818. It began as a religious reform movement to purge un-Islamic practices but soon became a social struggle as his son, Dudu Miyan, organized the peasantry against the exploitation of landlords and indigo planters.

questions.question: The commercialisation of agriculture under the British transformed the rural economy. What did 'commercialisation of agriculture' in British India lead to?
questions.solution:
Commercialisation of Agriculture

Commercialisation of agriculture under British rule led to the increased production of cash crops like cotton, indigo, tea, and opium for the global market. While this integrated India into world trade, it often led to a decrease in food grain production and made farmers vulnerable to market fluctuations.

questions.question: Which law allowed inter-caste and inter-religious marriages during the British period in India?
questions.solution:
The Act of 1872

The Special Marriage Act (Act III of 1872) was a landmark law that allowed inter-caste and inter-religious marriages. It provided a civil form of marriage for those who did not wish to marry under specific religious personal laws, requiring the parties to declare they did not profess any particular religion.

questions.question: Who was the Chief Architect behind the integration of over 560 princely states into the Indian Union?
questions.solution:
Sardar Vallabhbhai Patel

Sardar Vallabhbhai Patel is known as the 'Bismarck of India' for being the Chief Architect of the integration of over 560 princely states. Along with V.P. Menon, he used a mix of patriotism, diplomacy, and 'the stick' to ensure a unified Indian Union after independence.

questions.question: Which tribal revolt in 1831-32 was led by Buddho Bhagat and targeted exploitative landlords and British officials?
questions.solution:
Kol Revolt

The Kol Revolt (1831-32) was a major tribal uprising in the Chotanagpur region led by Buddho Bhagat. The Kols rose against the transfer of tribal lands to 'outsiders' (Sikh and Muslim farmers) and the oppressive judicial and revenue systems of the British.

questions.question: The infamous Jallianwala Bagh massacre occurred when Gandhi called all the Indians to observe April 6, 1919, as a day of non-violent protest against which of the following colonial policies?
questions.solution:
The Rowlatt Act

The Jallianwala Bagh massacre was the tragic culmination of protests against the Rowlatt Act. Gandhi had called for a hartal on April 6, 1919, against this 'Black Act,' which gave the government emergency powers to detain political activists without trial.

questions.question: Which of the following leaders is associated with the concept of ‘Drain of Wealth’ theory?
questions.solution:
Dadabhai Naoroji

Dadabhai Naoroji is the leader associated with the 'Drain of Wealth' theory. In his book, Poverty and Un-British Rule in India, he explained how India’s wealth was being systematically transferred to Britain through salaries, pensions, and trade imbalances.

questions.question: When was the Mines Act enacted in India?
questions.solution:
The Mines Act

The modern Mines Act was enacted in 1952 to regulate the labor and safety of workers in mines. It replaced earlier colonial-era laws to provide better welfare, working hours, and health standards for the mining workforce in independent India.

questions.question: What was Gandhi’s view about the relation between means and ends in Satyagraha?
questions.option_1: Violence is necessary for pure ends.
questions.option_4: Only ends matter, not the means.
questions.solution:
Means and Ends in Satyagraha

In Gandhian philosophy, the relationship between means and ends is inseparable. Gandhi believed that 'Pure ends require pure means'; he argued that one cannot achieve a just society through violent or immoral methods, as the means used eventually shape the nature of the end result.

questions.question: The Kherwar movement was led by which charismatic leader?
questions.solution:
Bhagirath Manjhi

The Kherwar (or Sapha Hor) movement of the 1870s was led by Bhagirath Manjhi. He called for a return to original Santhal traditions and the worship of one God (Sing Bonga), while also urging the Santhals to stop paying taxes to the British.

questions.question: Which Act ended the monopoly of the East India Company over trade with India?
questions.solution:
Charter Act of 1813

The Charter Act of 1813 ended the commercial monopoly of the East India Company over trade with India, opening the Indian market to all British merchants (except for trade in tea and trade with China, which were abolished later in 1833).

questions.question: During the 1857 Revolt, Indian sepoys declared which of the following as their symbolic leader?
questions.solution:
Bahadur Shah II

During the 1857 Revolt, the sepoys marched to Delhi and declared the octogenarian Mughal Emperor Bahadur Shah II (Bahadur Shah Zafar) as their symbolic leader. This provided the uprising with a semblance of national legitimacy and a central authority figure.

questions.question: The Non-cooperation Movement in 1920 included all the following, EXCEPT:
questions.solution:
Non-cooperation Movement

The Non-cooperation Movement (1920-22) was built on the principle of Ahimsa (non-violence). While it involved boycotting courts, schools, and taxes, violent attacks on officials were strictly against Gandhi’s directives; when violence occurred at Chauri Chaura, he called off the movement.

questions.question: Which reformer is known for his efforts to promote widow remarriage and women’s education in Bengal?
questions.solution:
Ishwar Chandra Vidyasagar

Ishwar Chandra Vidyasagar was the key reformer in Bengal who used ancient scriptures to prove that widow remarriage was sanctioned. His efforts led to the 1856 Act, and he also established numerous schools for women, often at his own expense.

questions.question: On which of the following dates did Mahatma Gandhi begin a 21-day fast of self-purification to support the Harijan movement?
questions.solution:
Harijan Movement Fast

On 8 May 1933, Mahatma Gandhi began a 21-day fast of self-purification while in Yerwada Jail. The fast was intended to emphasize the removal of untouchability and to motivate his followers to work more sincerely for the Harijan (Dalit) cause.

questions.question: The first session of the Indian National Congress was presided by ______.
questions.solution:
W.C. Bonnerjee

The first session of the Indian National Congress was presided over by Womesh Chandra Bonnerjee in December 1885. Held at Gokuldas Tejpal Sanskrit College in Bombay, the session was attended by 72 delegates who discussed the future political direction of India.

questions.question: Which of the following could be regarded as the only positive outcome of the British reforms in India?
questions.solution:
Positive Outcome of British Reforms

While the British rule was primarily exploitative, historians often regard social reform laws (such as the abolition of Sati, legalization of widow remarriage, and raising the age of consent) as the only positive outcomes that helped modernize Indian social thought.

questions.question: Hool Diwas is celebrated on June 30 every year in India to commemorate the Santhal Hool (rebellion) of _________.
questions.solution:
Hool Diwas

Hool Diwas is celebrated on June 30 to commemorate the start of the Santhal Rebellion (Hool) in 1855. Leaders Sidhu and Kanhu Murmu mobilized over 60,000 Santhals to revolt against the British East India Company and the oppressive zamindari system.

questions.question: In which of the following years was the town of Madrasapatnam, which later expanded and developed into modern-day Chennai, purchased by the East India Company (EIC) from local kings?
questions.solution:
Foundation of Madras

The town of Madrasapatnam was purchased by the East India Company's Francis Day and Andrew Cogan in 1639 from the Nayak rulers. This grant led to the construction of Fort St. George, the first major English settlement on the Coromandel Coast.

questions.question: Who is regarded as the chief architect of India’s Green Revolution?
questions.solution:
M.S. Swaminathan

M.S. Swaminathan is regarded as the Chief Architect of the Green Revolution in India. His scientific leadership in developing and introducing high-yielding varieties of wheat and rice transformed India from a food-deficient nation to a self-sufficient one in the late 1960s.
